title: Cacalia muricata
intro: Cacalia muricata, also known as the prickly cocklebur or prickly burreed, is a plant species in the family Asteraceae, characterized by its prickly stems and hairy leaves. Traditionally, Cacalia muricata has been used in herbal medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and digestive issues, due to its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antipyretic properties. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Cacalia muricata is often used in herbal preparations, such as teas, infusions, and tinctures, and is sometimes used as a substitute for other plants, such as Dandelion, due to its similar properties.
taxonomy: Cacalia muricata is a member of the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. This family is a large and diverse group of flowering plants, consisting of over 23,000 species. Within the Asteraceae family, Cacalia muricata is further classified as a member of the tribe Cardueae or the tribe Eupatorieae, but more precisely within the subfamily Asteroideae. The exact classification of Cacalia muricata can also vary depending on the source, but it is generally accepted as a species within the genus Cacalia. The genus Cacalia is a relatively small group of flowering plants native to East Asia.
